title: The Last Lesson by Alphonse Daudet
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Loss of language equals loss of identity. The chapter highlights patriotism, regret, and the importance of mother tongue.

summary:
The story is set in 1870 in the districts of Alsace and Lorraine in France, during the Franco-Prussian War. The narrator is Franz, a schoolboy who is late for school and afraid of being scolded by his French teacher, M. Hamel, because he has not prepared his lesson on participles. As he hurries past the town hall, he sees a crowd reading the bulletin board, where all bad news had come from during the war.

At school, everything feels strange. The classroom is silent, and village elders like old Hauser, the former mayor, and the former postmaster are sitting on the back benches. M. Hamel, dressed in his formal Sunday clothes, announces that it is their last French lesson. An order has come from Berlin: only German will be taught in the schools of Alsace and Lorraine. Franz is shocked and regrets not learning French seriously. He realizes how precious his language is only when he is about to lose it.

M. Hamel talks about the French language being the most beautiful, clear, and logical language in the world. He says that when people are enslaved, holding onto their language is like having the key to their prison. He blames everyone, including parents and himself, for neglecting French. He teaches the last lesson with great patience. When the church bells strike twelve and Prussian soldiers are heard, M. Hamel is overcome with emotion. He writes “Vive La France!” on the board and dismisses the class with a gesture.

qa.q: What was Franz expected to be prepared with for school that day?
qa.a: Franz was expected to be prepared with a lesson on participles. His French teacher, M. Hamel, had announced that he would question the class on this specific grammar topic. However, Franz did not know even the very first word about them and was deeply afraid of getting scolded.

qa.q: What did Franz notice that was unusual about the school that day?
qa.a: The school was completely quiet, resembling a calm Sunday morning instead of the usual morning hustle. There was no loud noise of opening and closing desks or lessons being repeated in unison. Most surprisingly, the back benches were occupied by sad village elders, and M. Hamel was wearing his beautiful Sunday clothes.

qa.q: What had been put up on the bulletin-board?
qa.a: An order from Berlin had been put up on the town hall bulletin-board. The notice declared that only German would be taught in the schools of Alsace and Lorraine. It effectively banned the teaching of the French language and announced the arrival of a new German master.

qa.q: What changes did the order from Berlin cause in school that day?
qa.a: The order brought a sudden, grim solemnity to the entire school environment. M. Hamel dressed in his finest ceremonial attire to honour his last lesson. The village elders sat quietly at the back of the classroom to express gratitude for their language and forty years of M. Hamel's faithful service.

qa.q: How did Franz’s feelings about M. Hamel and school change?
qa.a: Franz experienced a sudden, emotional shift from dread to profound regret. His books, which earlier felt like a heavy nuisance, instantly felt like old friends he could not give up. He completely forgot about M. Hamel’s strictness and cranky nature, feeling heartbroken that his teacher was leaving forever.

qa.q: The people in this story suddenly realise how precious their language is to them. What shows you this? Why does this happen?
qa.a: The intense grief and dedication shown by both the students and the village elders show how deeply they value their language. Old Hauser brings his childhood primer to learn, and M. Hamel gives his absolute best, calling French the most beautiful and logical language in the world. This realization happens because the imminent threat of losing their native tongue makes them recognize its value as an irreplaceable symbol of their identity and freedom.

qa.q: Franz thinks, “Will they make them sing in German, even the pigeons?” What could this mean?
qa.a: This thought highlights the absurdity and cruelty of linguistic imposition by conquerors. It suggests that while human rulers can control people and enforce new languages by political force, they cannot dominate nature or dictate the natural voice of living beings. It reflects a child's innocent realization that a person's mother tongue is as natural and inseparable from them as cooing is to a pigeon.
